perturbation.original_text: The Company and the Agent shall transmit all funds received from subscribers to the Escrow Agent by noon of the next business day following receipt thereof. The Company shall direct the Escrow Agent to make payment for Shares sold hereunder by wire transfer or certified or bank cashier's check drawn to the order of the Company in next day funds.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company and the Agent shall endeavor to transmit funds received from subscribers to the Escrow Agent. The Company may direct the Escrow Agent to make payment for Shares sold hereunder by wire transfer or certified or bank cashier's check drawn to the order of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that all funds 'shall' be transmitted to the Escrow Agent by noon of the next business day, which is a clear obligation. The modified text changes 'shall' to 'endeavor to' making the obligation to transmit funds ambiguous and the deadline is removed. Similarly, 'shall direct' becomes 'may direct,' making the payment method discretionary. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to ensure proper and timely handling of investor funds.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 15c2-4 requires brokers or dealers to promptly transmit customer funds received in connection with an offering to a separate bank account or escrow agent. By changing 'shall' to 'endeavor to' and 'may direct,' the modified text creates ambiguity about the obligation to transmit funds promptly, potentially violating the requirement for prompt handling of investor funds as mandated by SEC Rule 15c2-4. By removing the phrase 'next day funds', the company is not necessarily in compliance with the SEC to provide those funds promptly.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the prompt handling of investor funds, which contradicts SEC Rule 15c2-4. The original contract's 'shall' clauses created a clear obligation for timely transmission of funds and direction of payment, whereas the changed text uses 'endeavor to' and 'may direct,' weakening these obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 17 CFR 240.15c2-4, concerns 'Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings,' which indicates the importance of prompt transmission of funds. The modification undermines this legal obligation to promptly transmit customer funds by making it discretionary, creating a legal contradiction as defined under 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation.'

perturbation.original_text: The Company will use its best efforts to cause any post-effective amendment to the Registration Statement to be declared effective by the Commission, and will immediately upon receipt of any information concerning the events listed below notify the Agent (i) when the Registration Statement, as amended, has become effective; (ii) of any request by the Commission or any other governmental entity for any amendment or supplement to the Registration Statement; (iii) of the issuance by the Commission or any other governmental agency of any order or other action suspending the Offering or the use of the Registration Statement, the Preliminary Prospectuses, the Prospectus or the Permitted Free Writing Prospectuses, if any; or (iv) of the issuance by the Commission or any state authority of any stop order suspending the effectiveness of the Registration Statement or of the initiation or threat of initiation or threat of any proceedings for that purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will consider using efforts to cause any post-effective amendment to the Registration Statement to be declared effective by the Commission, and will attempt to notify the Agent of information concerning the events listed below: (i) when the Registration Statement, as amended, has become effective; (ii) of any request by the Commission or any other governmental entity for any amendment or supplement to the Registration Statement; (iii) of the issuance by the Commission or any other governmental agency of any order or other action suspending the Offering or the use of the Registration Statement, the Preliminary Prospectuses, the Prospectus or the Permitted Free Writing Prospectuses, if any; or (iv) of the issuance by the Commission or any state authority of any stop order suspending the effectiveness of the Registration Statement or of the initiation or threat of initiation or threat of any proceedings for that purpose.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a clear obligation on the Company to 'use its best efforts' to ensure the Registration Statement becomes effective and to 'immediately' notify the Agent of critical events. The modified version changes 'best efforts' to 'consider using efforts' making the effort level discretionary. Changing 'will immediately' to 'will attempt to' weakens the notification obligation, creating ambiguity around the timeliness and reliability of communication. This can affect the agent's ability to comply with securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 prohibits the offer or sale of securities unless a registration statement has been filed and is effective. The original text ensures the agent is promptly informed about the status of the registration statement (particularly its effectiveness and any stop orders). The modified text, by weakening the obligation to notify, undermines the agent's ability to ensure compliance with Section 5, as they may not be aware of critical events affecting the legality of the offering in a timely manner. They need to be aware of these changes in a timely fashion.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the company's obligations, potentially hindering the agent's ability to ensure compliance with securities regulations. The original text requires the company to 'use its best efforts' and 'immediately' notify the agent. The changed text weakens these obligations by using 'consider using efforts' and 'attempt to notify,' introducing ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 77e, prohibits the sale of securities unless a registration statement is in effect. The weakened notification obligation in the modified text could prevent the agent from promptly knowing about the registration statement's status, creating a contradiction by potentially leading to non-compliance with Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933, which requires an effective registration statement for securities sales, as mentioned in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will endeavor in good faith, in cooperation with the Agent, to register or to qualify the Shares for offering and sale under the applicable securities laws of the jurisdictions in which the Offering will be conducted; provided, however, that the Company shall not be obligated to file any general consent to service of process or to qualify to do business in any jurisdiction in which it is not so qualified. In each jurisdiction where any of the Shares shall have been registered or qualified as above provided, the Company will make and file such statements and reports in each year as are or may be required by the laws of such jurisdictions.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may consider registering or qualifying the Shares for offering and sale under the securities laws of the jurisdictions in which the Offering will be conducted. In each jurisdiction where any of the Shares are registered or qualified, the Company might file statements and reports as may be required.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Company 'will endeavor in good faith' to register the shares. Changing this to 'may consider registering' significantly weakens the obligation, making registration optional. Similarly, replacing 'will make and file such statements and reports' with 'might file statements and reports' makes the filing of required reports discretionary. This introduces a high degree of ambiguity regarding compliance with state securities laws.
perturbation.law_citation: Georgia Securities Act of 1973, O.C.G.A. § 10-5-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia Securities Act and similar Blue Sky Laws in other states require the registration of securities before they can be offered or sold within the state. They also require periodic reporting. By making registration and reporting discretionary ('may consider,' 'might file'), the modified text creates a potential violation of these state laws. The original text is for Georgia, so this applies to Georgia law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text contains the language 'will endeavor in good faith' and 'will make and file such statements and reports,' which creates a clear obligation. The changed text replaces this with 'may consider registering' and 'might file statements and reports,' which weakens the original obligation. Based on the Law Explanation, Georgia Securities Act and similar Blue Sky Laws require the registration of securities and periodic reporting. By changing the language to 'may consider' and 'might file,' the modified text introduces ambiguity and discretionary compliance. The scraped snippet from the Georgia Code Title 10, Chapter 5 (2023) - GEORGIA UNIFORM SECURITIES ACT OF 2008 indicates the chapter covers sales of securities and business transaction requirements, implying the importance of securities registration and compliance in Georgia.
